content:
# Numbers Chapter 15 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 166–167; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 123–124 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `NUM_015_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_015_page_002.jpg`; `NUM_015_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_015_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — LAWS OF OFFERINGS, THE SABBATH-BREAKER, AND TASSELS:** Numbers 15 stands in striking juxtaposition with the catastrophic rebellion narrative of chapters 13–14. Immediately after YHWH sentences the exodus generation to die in the wilderness, he speaks laws that begin with "When you come into the land of your dwelling" (v. 2) — a quiet, devastating assurance that the promise to the next generation remains intact. The chapter divides into four sections: laws of grain and drink offerings accompanying animal sacrifices (vv. 1–16), the offering of first-dough (vv. 17–21), atonement for inadvertent sin by the community and the individual (vv. 22–31), the case of the Sabbath-breaker (vv. 32–36), and the command to make tassels (tzitzit) on garments (vv. 37–41). On the Leningrad Codex, the chapter begins on page 166 (folio 84 recto, continuing from chapter 14) and extends to page 167. The Vaticanus preserves this chapter across pages 123–124 (pa_0123–0124). There is a setumah (closed paragraph) break before v. 1, and open/closed breaks at vv. 17, 22, 32, and 37 — marking each legal unit. The chapter's placement after the spy narrative is theologically deliberate: the land is still promised; the Torah still applies; YHWH's covenant endures beyond rebellion.

---

## Laws of Offerings Accompanying Sacrifices (vv. 1–16)

**1** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

> **Key terms:** וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר (va-yedabber YHWH el Mosheh le'mor) = "And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying" — the standard divine speech formula. The vav-consecutive on the piel imperfect of דבר (dabbar, "to speak") continues the narrative sequence directly from the judgment oracle of chapter 14. The LXX reads: καὶ εἶπεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν λέγων ("and [the] Lord said to Moses, saying").

**2** "Speak [dabber] unto [el] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-say [ve-amarta] unto-them [alehem]: 'When [ki] you-come [tavo'u] unto [el] the-land of [erets] your-dwellings [moshvoteikhem] which [asher] I [ani] [am]-giving [noten] to-you [lakhem],

> **Key terms:** כִּי תָבֹאוּ אֶל־אֶרֶץ מוֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם (ki tavo'u el erets moshvoteikhem) = "when you come to the land of your dwellings" — the temporal כִּי (ki, "when") with the qal imperfect of בוא (bo, "to come") presupposes entry into the land as certain. מוֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם (moshvoteikhem, "your dwellings") from ישׁב (yashav, "to sit, dwell") — the noun form connotes permanent settlement. Placed immediately after the sentence of death upon the wilderness generation, this "when you come" is addressed to the next generation — YHWH's promise has not been annulled. אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי נֹתֵן לָכֶם (asher ani noten lakhem) = "which I am giving to you" — the participle נֹתֵן (noten, "giving") presents the gift as continuous and ongoing, not past or future but present-tense divine commitment. **LXX:** ὅταν εἰσέλθητε εἰς τὴν γῆν τῆς κατοικήσεως ὑμῶν ("when you enter into the land of your habitation").

**3** and-you-make [va-asitem] a-fire-offering [isheh] to-YHWH [la-YHWH], a-burnt-offering [olah] or [o] a-sacrifice [zevakh], to-fulfill [le-falle] a-vow [neder] or [o] as-a-freewill-offering [bi-ndavah] or [o] in-your-appointed-feasts [be-mo'adeikhem], to-make [la-asot] a-pleasing-aroma [re'akh nikhoakh] to-YHWH [la-YHWH], from [min] the-herd [ha-baqar] or [o] from [min] the-flock [ha-tson]—

> **Key terms:** וַעֲשִׂיתֶם אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה (va-asitem isheh la-YHWH) = "and you make a fire-offering to YHWH" — אִשֶּׁה (isheh, "fire-offering") from אֵשׁ (esh, "fire"), the general term for any offering consumed by fire on the altar. לְפַלֵּא נֶדֶר (le-falle neder) = "to fulfill a vow" — the piel infinitive of פלא (pala), here meaning "to make extraordinary / to fulfill" a vow. Some render "to make a special [vow]." אוֹ בִנְדָבָה (o vi-ndavah) = "or as a freewill-offering" — נְדָבָה (nedavah, "freewill-offering"), from נדב (nadav, "to be willing, volunteer"), denoting a spontaneous, unobligated offering. בְּמֹעֲדֵיכֶם (be-mo'adeikhem) = "in your appointed feasts" — מוֹעֵד (mo'ed, "appointed time/feast"), from יעד (ya'ad, "to appoint"). רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ (re'akh nikhoakh) = "a pleasing aroma" — the technical sacrificial formula: רֵיחַ (re'akh, "scent/aroma") + נִיחֹחַ (nikhoakh, "soothing/pleasing"), from נוח (nuakh, "to rest"). **LXX:** καὶ ποιήσετε κάρπωμα κυρίῳ, ὁλοκαύτωμα ἢ θυσίαν ("and you shall make a fruit-offering to [the] Lord, a whole-burnt-offering or a sacrifice").

**4** and-shall-bring-near [ve-hiqriv] the-one-bringing-near [ha-maqriv] his-offering [qorbano] to-YHWH [la-YHWH], a-grain-offering [minkhah] of-fine-flour [solet], a-tenth [issaron] [of an ephah], mixed [belulah] with-a-fourth of [bi-rvi'it] the-hin [ha-hin] of-oil [shamen];

> **Key terms:** וְהִקְרִיב הַמַּקְרִיב (ve-hiqriv ha-maqriv) = "and the one bringing near shall bring near" — the hifil perfect of קרב (qarav, "to come near") followed by the hifil participle as a substantive, creating a cognate construction: "the offerer shall offer." This is characteristic of Levitical legislation. מִנְחָה סֹלֶת עִשָּׂרוֹן (minkhah solet issaron) = "a grain-offering of fine flour, a tenth" — מִנְחָה (minkhah) is the grain/meal-offering; סֹלֶת (solet) = "fine flour"; עִשָּׂרוֹן (issaron) = "a tenth [of an ephah]," approximately 2.3 liters. בְּלוּלָה בִּרְבִיעִת הַהִין שָׁמֶן (belulah bi-rvi'it ha-hin shamen) = "mixed with a fourth of the hin of oil" — the הִין (hin) is an Egyptian-derived liquid measure, approximately 6 liters; a fourth = approximately 1.5 liters. **LXX:** σεμίδαλιν δέκατον τοῦ οιφι ("fine flour, a tenth of the ephah").

**5** and-wine [ve-yayin] for-the-drink-offering [la-nesekh], a-fourth of [revi'it] the-hin [ha-hin], you-shall-make [ta'aseh] upon [al] the-burnt-offering [ha-olah] or [o] for-the-sacrifice [la-zavakh], for-the-one [la-] lamb [keves] the-one [ha-ekhad].

> **Key terms:** וְיַיִן לַנֶּסֶךְ רְבִיעִית הַהִין (ve-yayin la-nesekh revi'it ha-hin) = "and wine for the drink-offering, a fourth of the hin" — נֶסֶךְ (nesekh, "drink-offering/libation"), from נסך (nasakh, "to pour out"). Wine poured out as a libation accompanies the burnt-offering. לַכֶּבֶשׂ הָאֶחָד (la-keves ha-ekhad) = "for the one lamb" — כֶּבֶשׂ (keves, "young ram/lamb"), the standard sacrificial animal for individual offerings. **LXX:** καὶ οἶνον εἰς σπονδὴν τὸ τέταρτον τοῦ ιν ("and wine for a libation, the fourth of the hin").

**6** Or [o] for-the-ram [la-ayil], you-shall-make [ta'aseh] a-grain-offering [minkhah] of-fine-flour [solet], two-tenths [shenei esronim] mixed [belulah] with-oil [be-shemen], a-third of [shelishit] the-hin [ha-hin];

> **Key terms:** אוֹ לָאַיִל (o la-ayil) = "or for the ram" — אַיִל (ayil, "ram"), the mature male sheep, a more valuable animal than the כֶּבֶשׂ (keves, "lamb"). The grain-offering increases proportionally: two-tenths of fine flour (approx. 4.6 liters) with a third of a hin (approx. 2 liters) of oil. The graduated scale (lamb → ram → bull) shows proportional accompaniment.

**7** and-wine [ve-yayin] for-the-drink-offering [la-nesekh], a-third of [shelishit] the-hin [ha-hin], you-shall-bring-near [taqriv] as-a-pleasing-aroma [re'akh nikhoakh] to-YHWH [la-YHWH].

> **Key terms:** תַּקְרִיב רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ לַיהוָה (taqriv re'akh nikhoakh la-YHWH) = "you shall bring near a pleasing aroma to YHWH" — the hifil imperfect of קרב (qarav), second person singular, maintaining the direct address to the individual worshiper.

**8** And-when [ve-khi] you-make [ta'aseh] a-son of [ben] the-herd [baqar] as-a-burnt-offering [olah] or [o] a-sacrifice [zevakh], to-fulfill [le-falle] a-vow [neder] or [o] peace-offerings [shelamim] to-YHWH [la-YHWH],

> **Key terms:** בֶּן־בָּקָר (ben baqar) = "a son of the herd" — i.e., a young bull. The Hebrew idiom "son of X" (בֶּן + noun) denotes membership in a class. שְׁלָמִים (shelamim, "peace-offerings") from שׁלם (shalam, "to be complete, at peace"), the fellowship sacrifice whose meat was shared between the offerer, the priest, and YHWH.

**9** and-he-shall-bring-near [ve-hiqriv] upon [al] the-son of [ben] the-herd [ha-baqar] a-grain-offering [minkhah] of-fine-flour [solet], three [sheloshah] tenths [esronim] mixed [belulah] with-oil [be-shemen], half [khatsi] the-hin [ha-hin];

> **Key terms:** שְׁלֹשָׁה עֶשְׂרֹנִים סֹלֶת בְּלוּלָה בַשֶּׁמֶן חֲצִי הַהִין (sheloshah esronim solet belulah va-shemen khatsi ha-hin) = "three tenths of fine flour mixed with oil, half the hin" — the scale is complete: lamb = one-tenth flour + one-quarter hin oil/wine; ram = two-tenths + one-third hin; bull = three-tenths + one-half hin.

**10** and-wine [ve-yayin] you-shall-bring-near [taqriv] for-the-drink-offering [la-nesekh], half [khatsi] the-hin [ha-hin] — a-fire-offering [isheh] of-a-pleasing-aroma [re'akh nikhoakh] to-YHWH [la-YHWH].

> **Key terms:** The drink offering for the bull is half a hin (approx. 3 liters) of wine — the largest measure in the series. The summarizing phrase אִשֵּׁה רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ לַיהוָה (isheh re'akh nikhoakh la-YHWH) = "a fire-offering of pleasing aroma to YHWH" closes each sacrificial unit.

**11** Thus [kakhah] shall-be-done [ye'aseh] for-the-one [la-] ox [shor] the-one [ha-ekhad], or [o] for-the-one [la-] ram [ayil] the-one [ha-ekhad], or [o] for-the-one-of [la-] the-flock [seh] among-the-young-rams [ba-khvasim] or [o] among-the-goats [ba-izzim].

> **Key terms:** כָּכָה יֵעָשֶׂה (kakhah ye'aseh) = "thus it shall be done" — the niphal imperfect of עשׂה (asah, "to do/make"), passive: "it shall be done." כָּכָה (kakhah, "thus, so") summarizes the graduated scale. שֶׂה (seh) = a generic term for a member of the flock (either sheep or goat). בַּכְּבָשִׂים אוֹ בָעִזִּים (ba-khvasim o va-izzim) = "among the lambs or among the goats" — specifying the two types of flock animals.

**12** According-to-the-number [ka-mispar] which [asher] you-shall-make [ta'asu], thus [kakhah] you-shall-do [ta'asu] for-each-one [la-ekhad], according-to-their-number [ke-misparom].

> **Key terms:** כַּמִּסְפָּר אֲשֶׁר תַּעֲשׂוּ כָּכָה תַּעֲשׂוּ לָאֶחָד כְּמִסְפָּרָם (ka-mispar asher ta'asu kakhah ta'asu la-ekhad ke-misparom) = "according to the number that you make, thus you shall do for each one, according to their number" — the proportional accompaniment applies per animal: each animal offered requires its own grain and drink offering. The repetition of מִסְפָּר (mispar, "number") enforces strict mathematical proportionality.

**13** Every [kol] native-born [ezrakh] shall-do [ya'aseh] thus [kakhah] these [et-elleh], to-bring-near [le-haqriv] a-fire-offering [isheh] of-a-pleasing-aroma [re'akh nikhoakh] to-YHWH [la-YHWH].

> **Key terms:** כָּל־אֶזְרָח יַעֲשֶׂה כָּכָה (kol ezrakh ya'aseh kakhah) = "every native-born shall do thus" — אֶזְרָח (ezrakh, "native-born") from זרח (zarakh, "to rise, sprout"), denoting one who is indigenous, a citizen born in the community. This establishes the baseline: native Israelites must follow these proportions.

**14** And-when [ve-khi] sojourns [yagur] with-you [ittekhem] a-sojourner [ger], or [o] whoever [asher] [is] in-your-midst [be-tokh'khem] for-your-generations [le-doroteikhem], and-he-makes [ve-asah] a-fire-offering [isheh] of-a-pleasing-aroma [re'akh nikhoakh] to-YHWH [la-YHWH] — as-you-do [ka-asher ta'asu], thus [ken] he-shall-do [ya'aseh].

> **Key terms:** וְכִי־יָגוּר אִתְּכֶם גֵּר (ve-khi yagur ittekhem ger) = "and when a sojourner sojourns with you" — the cognate construction גֵּר + יָגוּר (ger yagur) is the technical term for a resident alien dwelling within Israelite society. The גֵּר (ger, "sojourner") is not a passing traveler but a permanent resident of non-Israelite origin. כַּאֲשֶׁר תַּעֲשׂוּ כֵּן יַעֲשֶׂה (ka-asher ta'asu ken ya'aseh) = "as you do, thus he shall do" — complete ritual parity between the native-born and the sojourner. **LXX:** καὶ ἐὰν προσέλθῃ ὑμῖν προσήλυτος ("and if a proselyte comes near to you") — the LXX translates גֵּר as προσήλυτος (proselytos, "proselyte"), reflecting the later Jewish understanding of the ger as a convert.

**15** [As for] the-assembly [ha-qahal], one-statute [khuqqah akhat] [shall be] for-you [lakhem] and-for-the-sojourner [ve-la-ger] the-sojourning [ha-gar]; a-statute [khuqqat] of-perpetuity [olam] for-your-generations [le-doroteikhem]: as-you [ka-khem] [so shall] the-sojourner [ka-ger] be [yihyeh] before [lifnei] YHWH.

> **Key terms:** הַקָּהָל חֻקָּה אַחַת לָכֶם וְלַגֵּר הַגָּר (ha-qahal khuqqah akhat lakhem ve-la-ger ha-gar) = "the assembly — one statute for you and for the sojourner who sojourns" — קָהָל (qahal, "assembly/congregation"), the gathered community as a legal entity. חֻקָּה אַחַת (khuqqah akhat, "one statute") — emphatic: there is one law, not two. חֻקַּת עוֹלָם (khuqqat olam, "a statute of perpetuity") = "an everlasting ordinance." כָּכֶם כַּגֵּר יִהְיֶה לִפְנֵי יְהוָה (ka-khem ka-ger yihyeh lifnei YHWH) = "as you, [so shall] the sojourner be before YHWH" — the preposition כְּ (ke, "as/like") on both pronouns creates a legal equation: you = the sojourner, before YHWH.

**16** One-law [torah akhat] and-one-judgment [u-mishpat ekhad] shall-be [yihyeh] for-you [lakhem] and-for-the-sojourner [ve-la-ger] the-sojourning [ha-gar] with-you [ittekhem]."

> **Key terms:** תּוֹרָה אַחַת וּמִשְׁפָּט אֶחָד יִהְיֶה לָכֶם וְלַגֵּר הַגָּר אִתְּכֶם (torah akhat u-mishpat ekhad yihyeh lakhem ve-la-ger ha-gar ittekhem) = "one torah and one judgment shall be for you and for the sojourner who sojourns with you" — תּוֹרָה (torah, "law/instruction") and מִשְׁפָּט (mishpat, "judgment/ordinance") together constitute the full legal framework. The repetition of אֶחָד/אַחַת (ekhad/akhat, "one") from v. 15 hammers home the principle: one people, one law, one God. **LXX:** νόμος εἷς ἔσται καὶ δικαίωμα ἓν ἔσται ("one law shall be and one ordinance shall be").

---

## The Offering of First-Dough (vv. 17–21)

**17** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

**18** "Speak [dabber] unto [el] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-say [ve-amarta] unto-them [alehem]: 'In-your-coming [be-vo'akhem] unto [el] the-land [ha-arets] which [asher] I [ani] [am]-bringing [mevi] you [etkhem] there [shammah],

> **Key terms:** בְּבֹאֲכֶם אֶל־הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי מֵבִיא אֶתְכֶם שָׁמָּה (be-vo'akhem el ha-arets asher ani mevi etkhem shammah) = "in your coming to the land where I am bringing you" — the infinitive construct of בוא (bo) with the second person plural suffix. Again the participle מֵבִיא (mevi, "bringing") — YHWH's active agency in bringing them to the land, echoing v. 2. The adverb שָׁמָּה (shammah, "there/thither") adds directional emphasis.

**19** and-it-shall-be [ve-hayah] in-your-eating [be-okholkhem] from-the-bread of [mi-lekhem] the-land [ha-arets], you-shall-raise [tarimu] a-contribution [terumah] to-YHWH [la-YHWH].

> **Key terms:** וְהָיָה בַּאֲכָלְכֶם מִלֶּחֶם הָאָרֶץ תָּרִימוּ תְרוּמָה לַיהוָה (ve-hayah ba-akholkhem mi-lekhem ha-arets tarimu terumah la-YHWH) = "and it shall be, in your eating from the bread of the land, you shall raise a contribution to YHWH" — תְּרוּמָה (terumah, "contribution/heave-offering"), from רום (rum, "to be high, to raise"), literally "that which is lifted up" — a portion separated and elevated for YHWH. לֶחֶם (lekhem, "bread/food") here means bread in the literal sense: the first dough made from the land's grain.

**20** The-first of [reshit] your-dough [arisoteikhem], a-cake [khallah], you-shall-raise [tarimu] as-a-contribution [terumah]; as-the-contribution of [ki-terumat] a-threshing-floor [goren], thus [ken] you-shall-raise [tarimu] it.

> **Key terms:** רֵאשִׁית עֲרִסֹתֵכֶם חַלָּה תָּרִימוּ תְרוּמָה (reshit arisoteikhem khallah tarimu terumah) = "the first of your dough, a cake, you shall raise as a contribution" — רֵאשִׁית (reshit, "first, beginning, firstfruits") from ראשׁ (rosh, "head"). עֲרִיסָה (arisah, "dough, coarse meal"), a word occurring only here and in Ezek 44:30; Neh 10:38 — the kneading-trough dough before baking. חַלָּה (khallah, "cake/loaf"), the perforated cake or round bread. כִּתְרוּמַת גֹּרֶן (ki-terumat goren) = "as the contribution of a threshing-floor" — the analogy compares the first-dough offering to the grain contribution taken from the threshing floor. **LXX:** ἀπαρχὴν φυράματος ὑμῶν ἄρτον ἀφαίρεμα ἀφοριεῖτε αὐτό ("a firstfruit of your dough — a bread of a portion you shall set apart").

**21** From-the-first of [me-reshit] your-dough [arisoteikhem] you-shall-give [tittenu] to-YHWH [la-YHWH] a-contribution [terumah] for-your-generations [le-doroteikhem].

> **Key terms:** מֵרֵאשִׁית עֲרִסֹתֵיכֶם תִּתְּנוּ לַיהוָה תְּרוּמָה לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם (me-reshit arisoteikhem tittenu la-YHWH terumah le-doroteikhem) = "from the first of your dough you shall give to YHWH a contribution for your generations" — the verb נתן (natan, "to give") replaces רום (rum, "to raise") from v. 20, showing these are equivalent actions. לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם (le-doroteikhem, "for your generations") = this is a perpetual obligation.

---

## Atonement for Inadvertent Sin (vv. 22–31)

**22** And-when [ve-khi] you-err [tishgu] and-not [ve-lo] do [ta'asu] [all of] all [et-kol] these [ha-] commandments [mitsvot] the-these [ha-elleh] which [asher] YHWH spoke [dibber] unto [el] Moses [Mosheh],

> **Key terms:** וְכִי תִשְׁגּוּ וְלֹא תַעֲשׂוּ אֵת כָּל־הַמִּצְוֺת הָאֵלֶּה (ve-khi tishgu ve-lo ta'asu et kol ha-mitsvot ha-elleh) = "and when you err and do not do all these commandments" — the verb שׁגה (shagah, "to err, go astray, sin inadvertently") is the key term in this section. This is not willful rebellion but inadvertent failure — a distinction critical to the atonement system. The scope is comprehensive: "all these commandments" — any of the laws just given (and by extension, any divine command).

**23** [even] all [et kol] which [asher] YHWH commanded [tsivvah] unto-you [aleikhem] by-the-hand of [be-yad] Moses [Mosheh], from [min] the-day [ha-yom] which [asher] YHWH commanded [tsivvah] and-onward [va-hal'ah] for-your-generations [le-doroteikhem] —

> **Key terms:** אֵת כָּל־אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֲלֵיכֶם בְּיַד־מֹשֶׁה (et kol asher tsivvah YHWH aleikhem be-yad Mosheh) = "all that YHWH commanded unto you by the hand of Moses" — בְּיַד (be-yad, "by the hand of") = "through the agency of." מִן־הַיּוֹם אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה וָהָלְאָה (min ha-yom asher tsivvah YHWH va-hal'ah) = "from the day that YHWH commanded and onward" — the adverb וָהָלְאָה (va-hal'ah, "and beyond/onward") extends the validity of all commandments from Sinai forward through all generations.

**24** and-it-shall-be [ve-hayah], if [im] from-the-eyes of [me-einei] the-congregation [ha-edah] it-was-done [ne'estah] in-error [li-shgagah], then-shall-make [ve-asu] all [kol] the-congregation [ha-edah] one [par] young-bull [ben baqar] [as] a-burnt-offering [le-olah] for-a-pleasing-aroma [le-re'akh nikhoakh] to-YHWH [la-YHWH], and-its-grain-offering [u-minkhato] and-its-drink-offering [ve-nisko] according-to-the-ordinance [ka-mishpat], and-one [u-se'ir] male-goat [izzim] one [ekhad] [as] a-sin-offering [le-khattat].

> **Key terms:** מֵעֵינֵי הָעֵדָה (me-einei ha-edah) = "from the eyes of the congregation" — the sin was hidden from communal awareness; it was committed unknowingly, "away from the eyes" of the assembly. לִשְׁגָגָה (li-shgagah) = "in error/inadvertently" — the noun שְׁגָגָה (shegagah, "inadvertence, mistake") from שׁגה (shagah). The remedy: one young bull as a burnt-offering (with its accompanying grain and drink offerings per vv. 8–10) and one male goat as a sin-offering (חַטָּאת, khattat, from חטא, khata, "to miss, sin"). **LXX:** adds that the congregation offers μόσχον ἕνα ἐκ βοῶν εἰς ὁλοκαύτωμα ("one calf from the herd as a whole-burnt-offering").

**25** And-shall-make-atonement [ve-khipper] the-priest [ha-kohen] upon [al] all [kol] the-congregation of [adat] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-it-shall-be-forgiven [ve-nislakh] to-them [lahem]; for [ki] an-error [shegagah] it [hi], and-they [ve-hem] brought [hevi'u] their-offering [et-qorbanam], a-fire-offering [isheh] to-YHWH [la-YHWH], and-their-sin-offering [ve-khattatam] before [lifnei] YHWH, upon [al] their-error [shiggatam].

> **Key terms:** וְכִפֶּר הַכֹּהֵן (ve-khipper ha-kohen) = "and the priest shall make atonement" — the piel perfect of כפר (kafar/kippur, "to cover, atone, make propitiation"). This verb is the theological center of the sacrificial system. וְנִסְלַח לָהֶם (ve-nislakh lahem) = "and it shall be forgiven to them" — the niphal perfect of סלח (salakh, "to forgive"), a verb used exclusively with God as the (implied) subject of forgiveness. כִּי שְׁגָגָה הִוא (ki shegagah hi) = "for it [is] an error" — the causal כִּי (ki, "for/because") explains why atonement is available: the sin was unintentional.

**26** And-it-shall-be-forgiven [ve-nislakh] to-all [le-khol] the-congregation of [adat] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] and-to-the-sojourner [ve-la-ger] the-sojourning [ha-gar] in-their-midst [be-tokham]; for [ki] to-all [le-khol] the-people [ha-am] [it was done] in-error [bi-shgagah].

> **Key terms:** וְנִסְלַח לְכָל־עֲדַת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְלַגֵּר הַגָּר בְּתוֹכָם (ve-nislakh le-khol adat benei Yisra'el ve-la-ger ha-gar be-tokham) = "and it shall be forgiven to all the congregation of the Sons of Israel and to the sojourner who sojourns among them" — the scope of forgiveness is identical to the scope of the law (cf. vv. 15–16): both native and sojourner receive atonement. כִּי לְכָל־הָעָם בִּשְׁגָגָה (ki le-khol ha-am bi-shgagah) = "for to all the people [it was] in error" — the entire community, including the sojourner, acted in ignorance.

**27** And-if [ve-im] a-soul [nefesh] one [akhat] sins [tekheta] in-error [bi-shgagah], then-she-shall-bring-near [ve-hiqrivah] a-she-goat [ez] a-daughter of [bat] her-year [shenatah] [as] a-sin-offering [le-khattat].

> **Key terms:** וְאִם־נֶפֶשׁ אַחַת תֶּחֱטָא בִשְׁגָגָה (ve-im nefesh akhat tekheta vi-shgagah) = "and if one soul sins in error" — the shift from community (vv. 24–26) to individual (vv. 27–29). נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh, "soul/person/life"), the individual as a living being. עֵז בַּת־שְׁנָתָהּ (ez bat shenatah) = "a she-goat, a daughter of her year" — i.e., a yearling female goat. The idiom "daughter of her year" (בַּת־שְׁנָתָהּ) means "in its first year" — a young animal.

**28** And-shall-make-atonement [ve-khipper] the-priest [ha-kohen] upon [al] the-soul [ha-nefesh] the-erring [ha-shogeget], in-her-sinning [be-khet'ah] in-error [bi-shgagah] before [lifnei] YHWH, to-make-atonement [le-khapper] upon-her [alav]; and-it-shall-be-forgiven [ve-nislakh] to-him [lo].

> **Key terms:** הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַשֹּׁגֶגֶת (ha-nefesh ha-shogeget) = "the erring soul" — the qal active participle of שׁגג (shagag, "to err"), feminine, modifying נֶפֶשׁ. בְּחֶטְאָה בִשְׁגָגָה (be-khet'ah vi-shgagah) = "in her sinning in error" — the infinitive construct of חטא (khata) with third feminine suffix. The priest makes atonement (כפר, kafar) and forgiveness (סלח, salakh) follows — the fixed theological sequence: sacrifice → priestly mediation → divine forgiveness.

**29** The-native-born [ha-ezrakh] among-the-Sons of [bi-vnei] Israel [Yisra'el] and-the-sojourner [ve-la-ger] the-sojourning [ha-gar] in-their-midst [be-tokham] — one-law [torah akhat] shall-be [yihyeh] for-you [lakhem], for-the-one-who-does [la-oseh] in-error [bi-shgagah].

> **Key terms:** הָאֶזְרָח בִּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְלַגֵּר הַגָּר בְּתוֹכָם תּוֹרָה אַחַת יִהְיֶה לָכֶם לָעֹשֶׂה בִּשְׁגָגָה (ha-ezrakh bi-vnei Yisra'el ve-la-ger ha-gar be-tokham torah akhat yihyeh lakhem la-oseh bi-shgagah) = "the native-born among the Sons of Israel and the sojourner who sojourns among them — one law shall be for you, for the one who acts in error" — this is the third repetition of the "one law" principle in this chapter (cf. vv. 15–16), now applied specifically to inadvertent sin: the same atonement procedure serves both native and alien.

**30** And-the-soul [ve-ha-nefesh] which [asher] does [ta'aseh] with-a-high [be-yad] hand [ramah], from [min] the-native-born [ha-ezrakh] and-from [u-min] the-sojourner [ha-ger] — YHWH [et-YHWH] he [hu] [is]-blaspheming [megaddef]; and-shall-be-cut-off [ve-nikhretah] the-soul [ha-nefesh] the-that [ha-hi] from-the-midst of [mi-qerev] her-people [ammah].

> **Key terms:** וְהַנֶּפֶשׁ אֲשֶׁר־תַּעֲשֶׂה בְּיָד רָמָה (ve-ha-nefesh asher ta'aseh be-yad ramah) = "and the soul that does [it] with a high hand" — the idiom בְּיָד רָמָה (be-yad ramah, "with a high/uplifted hand") means "defiantly, presumptuously, brazenly" — the opposite of שְׁגָגָה (inadvertence). This is deliberate, willful transgression. אֶת־יְהוָה הוּא מְגַדֵּף (et-YHWH hu megaddef) = "YHWH he is blaspheming" — the piel participle of גדף (gadaf, "to revile, blaspheme"). The direct object marker אֶת (et) before YHWH is emphatic: it is YHWH himself whom the defiant sinner insults. וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא מִקֶּרֶב עַמָּהּ (ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi mi-qerev ammah) = "and that soul shall be cut off from the midst of her people" — the כרת (karat, "to cut off") penalty, the most severe communal sanction, meaning either execution, excommunication, or divine extirpation. **LXX:** ψυχὴ ἥτις ποιήσει ἐν χειρὶ ὑπερηφανίας ("a soul who acts with a hand of arrogance") — the LXX renders יָד רָמָה as χεὶρ ὑπερηφανίας ("hand of haughtiness/arrogance").

**31** For [ki] the-word of [devar] YHWH he-despised [bazah] and-his-commandment [ve-et-mitsvato] he-broke [hefar]; being-cut-off [hikkarot], she-shall-be-cut-off [tikkarot], the-soul [ha-nefesh] the-that [ha-hi] — her-iniquity [avonah] [is] in-her [bah]."

> **Key terms:** כִּי דְבַר־יְהוָה בָּזָה וְאֶת־מִצְוָתוֹ הֵפַר (ki devar-YHWH bazah ve-et-mitsvato hefar) = "for the word of YHWH he despised and his commandment he broke" — בָּזָה (bazah, "he despised") from בזה (bazah, "to despise, hold in contempt"); הֵפַר (hefar, "he broke/violated") from פרר (parar, "to break, annul"). The two verbs form a devastating pair: contempt + violation. הִכָּרֵת תִּכָּרֵת (hikkarot tikkarot) = "being cut off, she shall be cut off" — **the infinitive absolute construction**, the most emphatic form in Hebrew: the niphal infinitive absolute (הִכָּרֵת) precedes the niphal imperfect (תִּכָּרֵת), intensifying certainty: "she shall surely be cut off." עֲוֺנָהּ בָּהּ (avonah bah) = "her iniquity [is] in her" — she bears full responsibility; the guilt remains upon her. **LXX:** ἐκτριβήσεται ἡ ψυχὴ ἐκείνη, ἡ ἁμαρτία αὐτῆς ἐν αὐτῇ ("that soul shall be utterly destroyed; her sin [is] upon her").

---

## The Sabbath-Breaker (vv. 32–36)

**32** And-were [va-yihyu] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] in-the-wilderness [ba-midbar], and-they-found [va-yimtse'u] a-man [ish] gathering [meqoshesh] wood [etsim] on-the-day of [be-yom] the-Sabbath [ha-Shabbat].

> **Key terms:** וַיִּמְצְאוּ אִישׁ מְקֹשֵׁשׁ עֵצִים בְּיוֹם הַשַּׁבָּת (va-yimtse'u ish meqoshesh etsim be-yom ha-Shabbat) = "and they found a man gathering wood on the day of the Sabbath" — מְקֹשֵׁשׁ (meqoshesh, "gathering"), the polel participle of קשׁשׁ (qashash, "to gather, collect"), specifically gathering sticks or straw. The narrative follows immediately after the declaration that deliberate sin merits כרת (cutting off), providing a concrete case study. The man's act is wood-gathering — a form of labor — on the Sabbath, a direct violation of the Sabbath commandment (Exod 20:10; 31:14–15; 35:2–3).

**33** And-brought-near [va-yaqrivu] him [oto] the-ones-finding [ha-motse'im] him [oto] gathering [meqoshesh] wood [etsim], unto [el] Moses [Mosheh] and-unto [ve-el] Aaron [Aharon] and-unto [ve-el] all [kol] the-congregation [ha-edah].

> **Key terms:** וַיַּקְרִיבוּ אֹתוֹ הַמֹּצְאִים אֹתוֹ (va-yaqrivu oto ha-motse'im oto) = "and those who found him brought him near" — the hifil of קרב (qarav, "to bring near"), here used in its judicial sense: bringing a transgressor before the authorities for adjudication. The man is brought before Moses, Aaron, and the entire congregation — the full judicial assembly.

**34** And-they-placed [va-yannikhu] him [oto] in-the-guardhouse [ba-mishmar], for [ki] it-had-not [lo] been-declared [forash] what [mah] should-be-done [ye'aseh] to-him [lo].

> **Key terms:** וַיַּנִּיחוּ אֹתוֹ בַּמִּשְׁמָר (va-yannikhu oto ba-mishmar) = "and they placed him in the guardhouse/custody" — the hifil of נוח (nuakh, "to rest, set down"); מִשְׁמָר (mishmar, "guard-post, custody, confinement"), from שׁמר (shamar, "to guard"). כִּי לֹא פֹרַשׁ מַה־יֵּעָשֶׂה לּוֹ (ki lo forash mah ye'aseh lo) = "for it had not been declared what should be done to him" — the verb פָּרַשׁ (parash, "to declare, make distinct, specify"), used only here in this form in the Torah; the penalty for Sabbath-breaking had been stated as death (Exod 31:14–15; 35:2), but the mode of execution had not been specified. The man is held in custody until YHWH clarifies the sentence. Cf. Lev 24:12, where a blasphemer is held similarly.

**35** And-said [va-yomer] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh]: "Dying [mot], the-man [ha-ish] shall-die [yumat]; stoning [ragom], all [kol] the-congregation [ha-edah] [shall stone] him [oto] with-stones [ba-avanim] outside [mi-khuts] the-camp [la-makhaneh]."

> **Key terms:** מוֹת יוּמַת הָאִישׁ (mot yumat ha-ish) = "dying, the man shall die" — **the infinitive absolute construction**: the qal infinitive absolute מוֹת (mot, "dying") + the hophal imperfect יוּמַת (yumat, "he shall be put to death"). This is the emphatic death sentence formula used throughout the Torah: "he shall surely be put to death." רָגוֹם אֹתוֹ בָאֲבָנִים כָּל־הָעֵדָה (ragom oto va-avanim kol ha-edah) = "stoning him with stones, all the congregation" — the qal infinitive absolute רָגוֹם (ragom, "stoning") specifies the mode of execution: communal stoning. מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה (mi-khuts la-makhaneh) = "outside the camp" — the execution takes place outside the camp to prevent contamination of the holy space. **LXX:** θανάτῳ θανατούσθω ὁ ἄνθρωπος ("by death let the man be put to death").

**36** And-brought-out [va-yotsi'u] him [oto] all [kol] the-congregation [ha-edah] unto [el] outside [mi-khuts] the-camp [la-makhaneh], and-they-stoned [va-yirgemu] him [oto] with-stones [ba-avanim] and-he-died [va-yamot], as-which [ka-asher] YHWH commanded [tsivvah] [to] Moses [et-Mosheh].

> **Key terms:** וַיִּרְגְּמוּ אֹתוֹ בָאֲבָנִים וַיָּמֹת (va-yirgemu oto va-avanim va-yamot) = "and they stoned him with stones and he died" — the terse narrative style: no emotional commentary, no protest, no elaboration. The community acts; the man dies; YHWH's command is fulfilled. כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת־מֹשֶׁה (ka-asher tsivvah YHWH et Mosheh) = "as YHWH commanded Moses" — the concluding formula of obedience.

---

## The Command of Tassels / Tzitzit (vv. 37–41)

**37** And-said [va-yomer] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

**38** "Speak [dabber] unto [el] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-say [ve-amarta] unto-them [alehem], and-they-shall-make [ve-asu] for-themselves [lahem] a-tassel [tsitsit] upon [al] the-corners of [kanfei] their-garments [vigdeihem] for-their-generations [le-dorotam]; and-they-shall-put [ve-natenu] upon [al] the-tassel of [tsitsit] the-corner [ha-kanaf] a-thread of [petil] blue [tekhelet].

> **Key terms:** וְעָשׂוּ לָהֶם צִיצִת עַל־כַּנְפֵי בִגְדֵיהֶם (ve-asu lahem tsitsit al kanfei vigdeihem) = "and they shall make for themselves a tsitsit upon the corners of their garments" — צִיצִת (tsitsit, "tassel, fringe"), from ציץ (tsits, "to blossom, sprout"); the word suggests something that protrudes or blossoms forth from the edge of the garment. כָּנָף (kanaf, "wing, corner, edge") — the "wings" of the garment, its four corners. פְּתִיל תְּכֵלֶת (petil tekhelet) = "a thread of blue/violet" — פְּתִיל (petil, "cord, thread"); תְּכֵלֶת (tekhelet), the prized blue-violet dye extracted from the murex snail (Bolinus brandaris), used also in the Tabernacle textiles and the high priest's garments. The blue thread on the tassel serves as a visual mnemonic — a reminder woven into daily dress. **LXX:** καὶ ποιήσουσιν ἑαυτοῖς κράσπεδα ἐπὶ τὰ πτερύγια τῶν ἱματίων αὐτῶν ("and they shall make for themselves fringes upon the edges of their garments") — κράσπεδα (kraspeda, "fringes/borders/tassels") translates צִיצִת; πτερύγια (pterygia, "little wings") translates כַּנְפֵי.

**39** And-it-shall-be [ve-hayah] for-you [lakhem] as-a-tassel [le-tsitsit], and-you-shall-see [u-re'item] it [oto] and-you-shall-remember [u-zkhartem] all [et-kol] the-commandments of [mitsvot] YHWH, and-you-shall-do [va-asitem] them [otam]; and-not [ve-lo] shall-you-spy-out [taturu] after [akharei] your-heart [levavkhem] and-after [ve-akharei] your-eyes [eineikhem] which [asher] you [attem] whoring [zonim] after-them [akhareihem].

> **Key terms:** וּרְאִיתֶם אֹתוֹ וּזְכַרְתֶּם אֶת־כָּל־מִצְוֺת יְהוָה (u-re'item oto u-zkhartem et kol mitsvot YHWH) = "and you shall see it and you shall remember all the commandments of YHWH" — the chain of perception: see → remember → do. The tsitsit is a visible anchor for memory. וְלֹא תָתֻרוּ אַחֲרֵי לְבַבְכֶם וְאַחֲרֵי עֵינֵיכֶם (ve-lo taturu akharei levavkhem ve-akharei eineikhem) = "and you shall not spy out after your heart and after your eyes" — the verb תּוּר (tur, "to spy out, explore, search") is the same verb used for the spies' mission in 13:2 (לָתוּר אֶת־הָאָרֶץ, "to spy out the land") — a deliberate verbal echo. The spies "spied out" the land and brought back faithless fear; now Israel is warned not to "spy out" after their own hearts and eyes. אֲשֶׁר אַתֶּם זֹנִים אַחֲרֵיהֶם (asher attem zonim akhareihem) = "which you [are] whoring after them" — the qal active participle of זנה (zanah, "to commit harlotry, to be unfaithful"), the prophetic metaphor of idolatry as sexual infidelity. The eyes and the heart lead to spiritual adultery; the tsitsit redirects the gaze to YHWH's commandments. **LXX:** καὶ οὐ διαστραφήσεσθε ὀπίσω τῶν διανοιῶν ὑμῶν καὶ τῶν ὀφθαλμῶν ὑμῶν ("and you shall not be turned away after your thoughts and your eyes").

**40** So-that [lema'an] you-shall-remember [tizkeru] and-you-shall-do [va-asitem] all [et-kol] my-commandments [mitsvotai], and-you-shall-be [vi-hyitem] holy [qedoshim] to-your-God [le-Eloheikhem].

> **Key terms:** לְמַעַן תִּזְכְּרוּ וַעֲשִׂיתֶם אֶת־כָּל־מִצְוֺתָי (lema'an tizkeru va-asitem et kol mitsvotai) = "so that you remember and do all my commandments" — the purpose clause: the tsitsit exists so that remembrance leads to obedience. וִהְיִיתֶם קְדֹשִׁים לֵאלֹהֵיכֶם (vi-hyitem qedoshim le-Eloheikhem) = "and you shall be holy to your God" — קָדוֹשׁ (qadosh, "holy, set apart"), the ultimate purpose of Torah observance: holiness — separation to God. This echoes the holiness code (Lev 19:2: "Be holy, for I, YHWH your God, am holy").

**41** I [ani] [am] YHWH your-God [Eloheikhem] who [asher] brought-out [hotseti] you [etkhem] from-the-land of [me-erets] Egypt [Mitsrayim] to-be [lihyot] for-you [lakhem] as-God [le-Elohim]: I [ani] [am] YHWH your-God [Eloheikhem]."

> **Key terms:** אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם אֲשֶׁר הוֹצֵאתִי אֶתְכֶם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם לִהְיוֹת לָכֶם לֵאלֹהִים (ani YHWH Eloheikhem asher hotseti etkhem me-erets Mitsrayim lihyot lakhem le-Elohim) = "I am YHWH your God who brought you out from the land of Egypt to be for you as God" — the exodus self-identification formula, echoing the Decalogue preamble (Exod 20:2). The hifil perfect of יצא (yatsa, "to go out"): הוֹצֵאתִי (hotseti, "I brought out"). לִהְיוֹת לָכֶם לֵאלֹהִים (lihyot lakhem le-Elohim) = "to be for you as God" — the purpose of the exodus was not merely liberation but covenant: YHWH freed Israel to be their God. אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם (ani YHWH Eloheikhem) = "I am YHWH your God" — the chapter ends as the Decalogue begins, forming an inclusio with the covenant identity of YHWH. **LXX:** ἐγὼ κύριος ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν ὁ ἐξαγαγὼν ὑμᾶς ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου εἶναι ὑμῶν θεός· ἐγὼ κύριος ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν ("I am [the] Lord your God, the one who brought you out from the land of Egypt, to be your God; I am [the] Lord your God").

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **Verse 2 — מוֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם (moshvoteikhem, "your dwellings"):** The Leningrad Codex reads the full plural with second masculine plural suffix. The LXX τῆς κατοικήσεως ὑμῶν ("of your habitation") is singular, which may reflect a different Vorlage or simply Greek idiom. The Hebrew plural "dwellings" emphasizes the breadth of settlement — multiple habitations across the land. I have followed the LC reading.

2. **Verse 3 — לְפַלֵּא (le-falle, "to fulfill/make extraordinary"):** This piel infinitive of פלא is unusual. Many translations render "to fulfill" or "to make special." The root פלא fundamentally means "to be wonderful/extraordinary." The LXX reads μεγαλῦναι (megalunai, "to magnify"), which captures the sense of making the vow conspicuous or extraordinary. I have rendered "to fulfill" as the contextual meaning, but the underlying Hebrew carries the connotation of making something exceptional or set apart.

3. **Verse 20 — עֲרִסֹתֵכֶם (arisoteikhem, "your dough"):** The noun עֲרִיסָה (arisah) is rare, occurring only here and in Ezek 44:30 and Neh 10:38. Its precise meaning has been debated since antiquity. The LXX renders φύραμα (phurama, "dough/lump of kneaded dough"). The Targumim render "your kneading-troughs." The Leningrad Codex vocalization clearly points to "dough" rather than "kneading-trough."

4. **Verse 24 — Communal sin-offering:** The MT prescribes one bull as a burnt-offering and one male goat as a sin-offering for communal inadvertent sin. Leviticus 4:13–21 prescribes a bull as a sin-offering for the same situation. This apparent discrepancy between Num 15:24 and Lev 4:13–21 is well-attested in rabbinic discussion (b. Horayot 7b–8a). The two passages likely address different circumstances or represent different strata of sacrificial legislation. I have translated the MT as it stands without harmonization.

5. **Verse 30 — בְּיָד רָמָה (be-yad ramah, "with a high hand"):** This idiom appears in Exod 14:8 and Num 33:3 to describe Israel's bold departure from Egypt — there it is positive (going out defiantly before Pharaoh). Here it describes the defiant sinner who transgresses deliberately. The same phrase carries opposite moral valence depending on whether the boldness is directed against Pharaoh or against YHWH. The LXX renders ἐν χειρὶ ὑπερηφανίας ("with a hand of arrogance"), interpreting the raised hand as haughtiness.

6. **Verse 31 — הִכָּרֵת תִּכָּרֵת (hikkarot tikkarot):** The infinitive absolute + imperfect construction. Per the EMET convention of preserving the infinitive absolute with doubling, I render: "being-cut-off, she-shall-be-cut-off." This is the emphatic form: the cutting-off is certain and absolute.

7. **Verse 34 — פֹרַשׁ (forash, "declared/specified"):** The pual perfect of פרשׁ (parash). This form occurs only here in the Torah. The verb means "to separate, distinguish, declare explicitly." The question was not whether Sabbath-breaking warranted death (that was clear from Exod 31:14–15; 35:2), but what mode of execution should be applied. YHWH's response in v. 35 specifies stoning.

8. **Verse 35 — Double infinitive absolute:** מוֹת יוּמַת (mot yumat, "dying he shall die") and רָגוֹם (ragom, "stoning") — two emphatic constructions in one verse. I preserve both doublings per EMET convention. Note that רָגוֹם stands alone as an infinitive absolute without a matching finite verb explicitly stated; the implied finite form is understood from the context: "stoning [shall stone] him."

9. **Verse 39 — תָתֻרוּ (taturu, "you shall spy out"):** The verbal connection to the spy narrative (Num 13:2, 17, 21, 25, 32; 14:6–7, 34, 36, 38) is unmistakable. The root תור (tur, "to spy, explore") binds the tsitsit command back to the catastrophic reconnaissance mission. The placement is not accidental: the chapter that begins with "when you come into the land" (v. 2) — the land the spies failed to trust YHWH to give — ends with a command to never again "spy out" after faithless hearts and wandering eyes. The tsitsit is the antidote to the sin of the spies.

10. **LXX Vaticanus (pa_0123–0124):** The Vaticanus text of Numbers 15 closely follows the Hebrew, with the main differences being: (a) the rendering of גֵּר (ger) as προσήλυτος (proselytos) rather than a more neutral term; (b) the use of κάρπωμα (karpoma, "fruit-offering") for אִשֶּׁה (isheh, "fire-offering") in some instances; (c) slight variations in the grain-offering measures that align with the Septuagintal tradition of using δέκατον (dekaton, "tenth") explicitly. No major textual variants that would alter translation were observed. The folios are in fair condition with some darkening along the inner margins of pa_0124 but all text remains legible.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), folios 84r–84v (pp. 166–167), with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0123–0124. — EMET*
